title: Card network catalogue
description: Every supported card network + its default chargeback fee range + evidence-deadline defaults + rate threshold. The valid_network rule reads this catalogue. Provider webhook handlers use it to normalize provider-native network identifiers.

networks.notes: Largest network globally. Rapid Dispute Resolution (RDR) + Compelling Evidence 3.0 (CE3.0) both supported. VDMP breach at 0.9% triggers merchant risk program enrollment.

Second-largest network globally. MDMP (Mastercard Dispute Monitoring Program) breach at 1.5% triggers merchant risk program. No RDR-equivalent currently supported (Mastercard's Dispute Resolution 2.0 has different mechanics; extend when supported). Longer evidence window than Visa (45 days vs 30).
Higher fees than Visa/Mastercard. Shorter evidence deadline (20 days). No RDR equivalent. Amex Enhanced Dispute monitoring at 1.0%.
Discover Merchant Dispute Program (MDP) monitoring at 1.0%. Similar mechanics to Mastercard but different scoring.
Japan-based network. Limited chargeback volume outside Japan + Asia-Pacific.
China-based network. Limited outside China + Asia-Pacific.
Diners Club International. Low volume; often issued through Discover Network in the US.
Catch-all for less common networks (RuPay, Elo, Cabal, regional networks). Uses conservative defaults. Provider communicates specific fee + deadline in webhook payload when known.

notes: iso_prefix_bin is informational — provider webhooks pass the network identifier directly; we don't do BIN lookups.
default_fee_cents is the fallback when provider webhook does not communicate the actual fee. Providers typically pass the exact fee.
default_evidence_deadline_days is the fallback. Provider webhook communicates the actual deadline (may be tighter during heavy dispute volume).
supports_rdr / supports_ce3 gate the RDR + CE3.0 paths at chargeback creation time. Only Visa currently supports either.
typical_decision_days_range is informational for admin UX (showing estimated decision window). Not enforced.
